content:
The library had always been Auri’s refuge. Tonight, she sat at one of the long oak tables near the back, books spread out in a careful semicircle around her like wards. Some were open, others stacked and tabbed, a few already containing notes stuffed in the pages written in her precise, compact script. At a glance, it looked orderly, intentional, and controlled. 

It wasn’t.

Her attention moved restlessly from page to page: a volume on individual founders of Spark Oath to fulfill the obligations bestowed upon her by Artorius; another about arcane rubies that might help Caspian contact her mentor; glamour spells for Sol and Bookie; and a novel she’d checked out specifically for her next meeting with Vornakir. She’d read the same paragraph twice without absorbing it, then caught herself and exhaled softly through her nose. 

Artorius’ task lingered on the edge of her thoughts: the book, *Termina Edict*, hidden in the cellar, the one he’d trusted her to find. There was the upcoming spell duel. Homework for classes. Part-time jobs and club meetings. And layered over all of it was the Chroma Clash, which demanded strategy and foresight.

Preparation, a good cup of tea, and her favorite corner of the library usually soothed her, but tonight, they only made the weight more noticeable. Auri caught herself biting her bottom lip and stilled, forcing her jaw to relax. Her tail, which had been curling and uncurling beneath the table, gave an impatient swish before she looped it around the leg of her chair to keep it still. 

Anyone passing by would see a composed scholar deep in study. Focused, capable, and unbothered. Anyone who knew her *well* would notice the tells: the way she worried her lip, the way she swished her tail, the untouched cup of tea cooling beside her for nearly an hour.

Auri turned another page, eyes scanning the text as if this time it would finally settle everything, but it didn’t. She leaned back slightly, gaze lifting toward the shelves, and let herself breathe. Long enough for the cracks to show, if anyone happened to be watching closely enough to notice. 

<@544474001288986624>

Redd slipped into the library with the ease of someone who knew exactly where he was going, and who he was looking for. He spotted her immediately. Back table. Semicircle of books. Tea gone untouched long enough to form its own little ecosystem. 

He slowed just a fraction, took it in, and gave a small, fond eye roll to himself.

He approached quietly, stopping just short of her table. The tail. The lip. The same paragraph read twice. Yep. That tracked.

Without announcing himself, he set two neatly wrapped bundles down beside her cooling cup of tea, careful not to disturb the meticulous chaos of her books. Only then did he speak, voice low and amused.

“I know,” he said mildly, “The food isn't as good as anything your *Ashvael* makes.”

A beat.

“But before you start composing a formal complaint in your head—” He nudged one of the bundles closer to her with a fingertip. “Longaniza breakfast burrito. Eggs, rice, the works. Still warm.”

He drew the other bundle of black beans and chickpeas a little closer to himself, a quiet, practiced motion, then glanced back at her. “I planned ahead,” he added lightly.

His gaze flicked over the spread of texts, then back to her face, expression softening without losing its lightness. “You’ve been here long enough that your tea’s entered its reflective era,” he added. “Figured I’d intervene before you tried to power through and exhaust yourself again before the clash.”

He glanced once more at the books, then back to her, a hint of a smirk tugging at his mouth. “Research spiral? Still looking for that forbidden book?”

Auri startled just slightly when the bundles appeared beside her tea, and the book hovering in front of her dropped with a soft *thump* onto the table. She looked up to see Redd standing there with an amused expression.

Her shoulders eased almost immediately, tension loosening in a way she hadn’t quite managed on her own. Still, at the mention of her nickname for Vornakir, a faint warmth crept into her cheeks before she could stop it. One of the books in front of her snapped closed with the flick of her finger. The cover read *Courtship of Eternal Blossoms*, depicting a couple gazing at one another amid a cascade of orchids.

She cleared her throat and lifted her chin, as if she absolutely was not flustered. “...You didn’t have to call me out like that, you know,” she said, though there was no heat behind her words— only fondness. “And I was *not* composing a formal complaint.”

 She picked up the bundle, unwrapping it with care before taking a bite. Her eyes closed for half a second in clear appreciation. “...Okay,” she conceded after swallowing. “It’s not as good as Vornakir’s cooking.” She shot Redd a quick, amused glance. “But it *is* very good. And significantly better than the granola bar I grabbed from the dining hall.”

At his comment about her tea, she followed his gaze and winced. “I hadn’t realized it had reached self-awareness.” She nudged the cup aside and took another bite, grounding herself in the simple act of eating, which was something she was very good at forgetting when she pushed herself like this. “Thank you,” she added quietly, sincerity slipping in beneath the banter. “Really. You’ve officially prevented me from pretending I don’t need food or a break.”

Then she gestured vaguely at the spread of books with her free hand. “Yes,” she said. “Research spiral. I’ve been researching everything I can about the founders, but every book carries the same information.” A small sigh escaped her. “And apparently everything else in my life decided now was a good time to demand attention too. Spell duels, studying, the Clash, helping Sol and Caspian, and—“ she paused as she thought about everything that had happened recently, a smirk playing at her lips as her tail gave a flick, “Sharing a first kiss with Vornakir.”

“So,” she finished lightly, but with an undercurrent of trust, “What about you? How are things going since you spoke with Zurr’ka? I noticed you’ve been involved in a lot of spell duels too.”

Redd listened without interrupting, attention fixed on her as she spoke, the easy humor in his posture giving way to something more focused. When she finished, he let out a quiet breath and shook his head once.

“That’s… a lot,” he said plainly. “And I’m impressed you’re still upright.”

His gaze drifted briefly to the books, then back to her—until she mentioned it.

The kiss.

He stilled, brows lifting slowly. Then he looked at her again, really looked this time, eyes narrowing with exaggerated scrutiny.

“Hold on,” he said. “We are not treating that like a casual aside.”

He leaned an elbow on the table, lowering his voice like this was suddenly classified information. “You don’t just slip ‘first kiss with Vornakir’ into a list between spell duels and errands.” A pause. “That’s HUGE!”

The intensity softened into something warm, genuine. “I’m glad,” he said simply. “About him and you. I think you're good for each other.”

He straightened—and the shift was almost imperceptible when he spoke again.

“As... As for Zurr’ka…” He stalled, eyes dropping to the burrito in his hands. His grip tightened slightly in the wrapper. “We’re taking a break.”

A beat. Then, quieter, edged with something tired and petulant, “Which I’m trying to be mature about. Truly. Gold star to me for personal growth.”

He huffed a breath through his nose, shaking his head. “But I don’t know if it’s a pause or just… I don't even want to think about the alternative.”

His gaze stayed fixed on the table as the words started to trip over one another. “I keep replaying everything. Timing. What I said. What I *didn’t* say. Whether this was always how it was going to go.” A humorless laugh slipped out. “First real relationship, and, surprise, it’s already falling apart.”

He finally looked up again, a crooked, self-defeating smile tugging at his mouth. “Feels a bit like confirmation of a long-standing theory about me.”

He took a bite of his food, grounding himself before continuing. “So I’ve been throwing myself into spell duels. Control where I can get it.” He glanced at her. “There’s one guy, Berrick Flintspine. The guy the court wants to pair me up with. Persistent. Annoying. Good enough to keep me on my toes.”

Then, deliberately, he set the spiral aside.
He nodded toward the founders’ books. “You said they’re all repeating the same story. That usually means something’s being preserved, or something’s being avoided.” His tone shifted back, supportive but clearly changing the topic. “I can help you sift it. Footnotes, variant editions, omissions. I won’t step on your work.”

Auri blinked at Redd’s enthusiastic reaction to the kiss comment, and despite herself, her smile widened. “Okay, in my defense,” she said lightly, “I was trying not to derail the entire conversation.” The warmth in her expression didn’t fade, and she let out a dreamy little sigh and rested her chin in her hands. “...but it was actually two kisses,” she admitted, her hands sliding up to her cheeks. “And I cannot stop thinking about it.”

She was about to gush a little more when she caught the shift. She didn’t interrupt when Redd mentioned the break. Instead, she listened, the playful energy fading from her posture as her focus sharpened. Her tail wrapped around her ankles, a clear sign that she was deep in thought. 

“A break is a pause, right? It doesn’t automatically mean that it’s over,” she said finally. “Did Zurr’ka say why?” Her gaze didn’t waver from his as she stacked her books to the side and made space for him to sit at the table with her, and if he allowed it, she took his hand in hers. 

“Throwing yourself into duels makes sense,” she added. “Just don’t get carried away, okay? Take care of yourself.” She lifted the burrito he made her. “You look after me, and I look after you too. That’s how this works.”

“As for the founders,” she added, taking another bite of her breakfast and nudging one of the books toward him. She swallowed, then continued. “You’re right. Repetition means preservation or omission. And I would appreciate the help. We’ll sort the founders, and then you’ll talk to Zurr’ka when he reaches out to you because you’re choosing not to run.” She tilted her head and smiled. “For now, you *are* being mature by giving him space. The gold star still stands.”

Redd stared at her for a long second after she said two.

“Two,” he repeated flatly, as if she had just confessed to a minor felony. “You were absolutely going to let me leave here thinking it was one.”

But he was smiling—helplessly, genuinely. The teasing softened almost immediately. “I’m glad,” he said, quieter. “You look… lighter talking about it.”

When she took his hand, the shift in him was subtle but real. His fingers tightened instinctively before easing, thumb brushing once over her knuckles like he was confirming she was actually there. He hadn’t realized how wound he’d been until that small contact steadied something in his chest.

At her optimism about breaks, he gave a small, uneven huff.

“A break is a pause,” he echoed, but it sounded more like he was testing a theory than agreeing with it. “He said things were intense. That he needed space.”

His jaw worked slightly.

“I can be intense,” he admitted. “I overthink. I push for reassurance. I try to fix things before they’ve even settled. And when I don’t get clarity, I spiral. Or I withdraw. Or I pick fights with something. Easier than facing the actual problem.”

A humorless breath escaped him. “This is my first real relationship. I don’t know what I’m doing. I’ve made so many mistakes already, and I keep replaying them like if I just analyze it hard enough I’ll find the exact sentence where I ruined it.”

His gaze dropped to their joined hands.

“Maybe I’m just… too much,” he said quietly. “Too intense. Too damaged for something that’s supposed to be simple.”

He swallowed, then added, softer still, “If he needed space, I probably made it impossible not to.

The words hung there.

Redd didn’t move immediately after he said them. He stayed still, staring at the table like he could will the confession back into something less raw.

For a moment, it looked like he might say more.
Instead, he let out a quiet breath and gave the smallest shake of his head, like he’d caught himself going too far.

“Anyway,” he muttered. “That’s enough self-awareness for one evening.”

He let out a small, self-conscious huff, like he could laugh it off. It didn’t quite land.
His fingers eased from hers with a slight hesitation, like part of him didn’t want to let go. When he did pull back, he rolled his shoulders once, settling into posture.

He didn’t fill the silence immediately.

Instead, he began separating identical paragraphs with practiced precision, building small piles, original printings, revised copies, annotated editions. His movements were calm, methodical. Grounded.

Auri went very still when Redd said it—  *Too much.* For a moment, she just watched him and the careful way he sorted the papers. Something sharp and protective sparked behind her ribs. “...Redd.” Her voice was quiet, but there was heat under it now. She leaned forward slightly, brows pulling together, tail giving one tight flick before going still. “You are *not* too much.” 

Her fingers curled against the table’s edge as she continued, more controlled than the emotion briefly flashing through her eyes. “You care deeply. You think ahead. You try to fix things before they break.” She shook her head. “Those are *not* flaws. That’s just…you refusing to not run away anymore.”

“I’m choosing my words carefully,” she added, kind but firm, “because I know Zurr’ka matters to you.” Auri took a slow breath, clearly trying to make sure her tone stayed where she wanted it. “But right now, you don’t have clarity. You have silence. Did he say how long this break would be or explain what ‘too intense’ even means? Does he realize that your brain is doing what brains do and filling in the worst possible explanation? Does he *care*?”

A flicker of something vulnerable crossed her face then. She hesitated, then more softly, “You remember my first relationship and how that turned out.” Her fingers twisted lightly in the hem of her skirt. “He thought I was too much, and to be fair…” a small, self-aware huff, “...I know I’m intense.” Her eyes lifted back to his. “But would you ever tell me I was too much…*for you*? You said that you thought Vornakir was good for me. Can you imagine him saying that to me?”

The question wasn’t defensive. It was quiet, trusting, and raw in a very ‘Auri’ way.” A beat passed, then she shook her head lightly, like she already knew the answer. “You don’t have to shrink yourself just because you’re scared of being hard to hold onto,” she said. “The right people don’t see you as ‘too much.’ They see you the way I do…as someone they would do *anything* not to lose.”

If Redd allowed it, she gently pulled him into an embrace. He was the first person since leaving the tower whom she had called a friend, and the first she trusted this deeply. Her eyes stung before she could stop it, her throat tight with the effort of holding steady for him. After a moment, she pulled back just enough to look at him, hands still resting lightly on his shoulders, her amber eyes glassy with tears.

“You’re my best friend,” she whispered softly, her voice catching just a little. “You know that, right?”

The words hit him harder than anything she’d said all night.

> The right people don’t see you as too much.

Redd’s practiced smile didn’t even get the chance to form this time.

It just collapsed.

His breath caught sharply in his chest, like he’d misjudged a step. He blinked once, twice; fast, irritated, as if he could blink the sting away.

He couldn’t.

When she pulled him into the embrace, he didn’t resist.

For half a second, he stayed stiff—like he didn’t know what to do with it. And then something in him gave way completely.

The first tear slipped before he could stop it.

Then another.

And then it wasn’t contained anymore.

Redd didn’t cry quietly.

It came in uneven, broken breaths—shoulders shaking, jaw clenched like he was embarrassed by the sound of it even as he couldn’t control it. 

He tried to clamp down on it. Bit down on the inside of his cheek. Dragged in a breath through his nose like that might force the tears back where they belonged.

The next sob hit worse, deep, shuddering, violent enough that it folded him forward against her. His whole body shook with it, breath splintering into gasps he couldn’t regulate. He made a small, desperate sound in the back of his throat like he was furious at himself for losing control and powerless to stop it.

“I—” The word shattered the moment it left him.
He squeezed his eyes shut, tears spilling freely now, hot and relentless. His face pressed harder into her shoulder, as if hiding would somehow make it smaller.

“I don’t know how to do this,” he sobbed, the confession ripped out between uneven breaths. “I don’t know how to be with someone and not ruin it—”

His grip tightened again, almost apologetically.

“I tried so hard not to mess this up,” he choked out. “I tried to be better. I tried not to be the version of me that leaves first.”

Another breath, sharp and shaky.

“And it still wasn’t enough.”

Her whisper undid him all over again.

“Of course I know that,” he managed, voice wrecked. Then, softer, trembling, “You’re mine too.”

His hands came up, cupping her wrists lightly like he needed to anchor himself to something solid.

“I just…” His throat worked again. “I don’t want to lose people anymore.”

And this time, there was no joke to hide behind.

Auri didn’t let go of Redd. The moment he folded into her, her arms tightened instinctively, one hand coming up to cradle the back of his head without hesitation. She felt every shudder that went through him. Every uneven breath. Every place he was trying— and failing— to hold himself together. 

Her chest ached.

Her thumb moved in a slow, absent sweep at the nape of his neck, gentle and rhythmic. When he choked out that it still hadn’t been enough, something in her expression finally cracked. Her brows pulled together, her eyes burned, and before she could stop it, she felt the sting spill over. 

Auri blinked hard, but it was too late. 

“....Redd,” she whispered, her voice threaded with something vulnerable she wasn’t bothering to hide. Carefully, she pulled back slightly to look at him, one arm still wrapped securely around him. The other lifted, and she gently brushed her thumb beneath his eye, wiping away the tear that had escaped. The touch was feather-light and reverent.

“You trying this hard isn’t failure,” she said quietly, her voice breaking slightly in different places. “It means you care, and that you are the person you wanted to be. The person who doesn’t run away. Sometimes….even when we try our best, things still get complicated.” 

Her voice wavered once before she steadied it again. When he admitted he didn’t want to lose people, her expression softened completely, something warm and fiercely protective settling into place.

This time, when she pulled him back into the hug, it was just a little tighter. Her cheek rested lightly against his hair as she spoke. “You’re not losing me.” Her fingers curled lightly in the fabric at his shoulder. “I’m right here,” she continued softly. “And I’m not going anywhere. You don’t have to earn your way into being worth staying for. I love you far too much to ever let you go.” She pulled back just enough to look at him again, and her eyes were unmistakably glassy, bur her smile was small and fond.
